macro_segments.title: The Secondary Forbidden Relationships (Shniyot L'Arayot): Great-Aunts and the List of Sixteen

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Question of Great-Aunts (Grandfather's Sister and Grandfather's Brother's Wife)
Debate: Is the Prohibition of Great-Aunts Derived by Analogy (Gezeira) from the Father's Generation?
The Mishna Does Not List Great-Aunts; Appeal to the Sixteen Secondary Arayot
Ameimar Permits Great-Aunts; Rav Hillel's Evidence of Sixteen Forbidden Secondary Arayot
Counting to Sixteen: Eight from Yesterday's List, Six from Rabbi Chiya, and Two Great-Aunts
Objection: Should Be Seventeen Due to the Mother's Brother on the Maternal Side; Resolution via Similarity
Rav Hillel's Source is Not Authoritative; Cannot Rely on it to Establish Halakha

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya's Expansion of Secondary Arayot: Great-Granddaughters and Great-Grandmothers

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Two-Generation Prohibitions from Torah (Wife's Grandmother and Your Granddaughter)
Rabbi Chiya's Innovation: Great-Granddaughters (Third Generation Removed from You)
Rabbi Chiya's Second Set: Your Wife's Great-Granddaughters (Whether Through Son or Daughter)
Rabbi Chiya's Third Set: Your Wife's Great-Grandmother (Fourth Generation Ascending)
Terminology Debate: Why Count Generations Differently for Ascending vs. Descending Relationships?
Do Rabbi Chiya's Prohibited Relatives Have a Cutoff, or Do Prohibitions Continue Indefinitely?

macro_segments.title: Application of Secondary Arayot Prohibitions to Converts (Gerim)

micro_segments.title: Opening Question: Does the Principle of Kegar Shenithgayyer Apply to Secondary Arayot?
Eretz Yisrael Tradition: Converts May Marry Secondary Relatives Because Non-Jews Have No Such Prohibitions
Biblical Arayot for Converts: Avoided Due to Perception of Judaism as Less Holy Than Non-Jewish Religion
Secondary Arayot for Converts: Permitted Because No Appearance of Leniency

macro_segments.title: Testimony of Converted Brothers: Maternal vs. Paternal Relatives

micro_segments.title: Maternal Brothers Who Converted Cannot Testify Together Lechatchila; Permitted Bediavad
Paternal Brothers Who Converted May Testify Together Lechatchila
Rationale: Maternal Relationship Shows Clear Biological Bond; Paternal Relationship Less Obvious
Even Maternal Brothers May Testify Together Lechatchila Because Beit Din Knows Converts Are Unrelated
Arayot vs. Testimony: Different Concerns—Personal Choice vs. Court-Controlled Judgment

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of a Brother or Son for Yibum and Pesul Status

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Any Type of Brother Creates Yibum Obligation; Excluded Only: Half-Brother from Non-Jewish Woman or Slave
Gemara: Does a Mamzer Brother Count? Yes, Because He Has Yibum Relevance as a Son-Type Category
Mamzer Is Considered Brother for All Purposes: Inheritance and Priestly Impurity (Tamei)
Kashering Analogy: Priest's Impurity to His Wife Depends on Her Status; So Too for Brother and Mamzer

macro_segments.title: Sons (Including Mamzerim) Exempt the Father's Wife from Yibum

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Any Type of Son Exempts Father's Wife from Yibum; Only Excluded: Son from Non-Jewish Woman or Slave
Gemara: Mamzer Son Exempts Father's Wife; Derived from 'Ein Lo'—Examine What Counts as a Son
Expansion: Daughters, Grandsons, and Granddaughters Also Exempt; Not Only Sons
Mamzer Son and the Obligation to Honor Father: Must Father Have Done Teshuva?
Resolution: Mamzer Father Need Not Achieve Full Atonement; Must Simply Act Observantly (Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha)

macro_segments.title: Talmudic Debate on Double Transgression: Sister vs. Sister Who Is Father's Wife's Daughter

micro_segments.title: Baraita: One Who Has Relations with Sister Who Is Father's Wife's Daughter Commits Two Prohibitions
Tanna Kamma: Two Separate Transgressions—Sister and Father's Wife's Daughter
Rabbi Yose bar Yehuda: Only One Prohibition—Sister; Father's Marriage to Mother Does Not Create New Issur
Tanna Kamma's Reasoning: Redundancy in Two Verses Teaches Two Distinct Prohibitions
Rabbi Yose's Reasoning: Phrase 'Achosecha Hi' Limits to Single Prohibition; No New Issur from Father's Marriage
Tanna Kamma Responds: 'Achosecha Hi' Is Needed to Teach Full Sister; Otherwise Derived by Kal Va'chomer
Rabbi Yose Responds: If 'Achosecha Hi' Meant Full Sister, Why Place It in Verse About Father's Marriage?
